content.body: Source URL doesn't necessarily need to be the source of identity, but Source Text only has its own problems, especially if a site isn't properly secured and can load arbitrary javascript. they could theoretically import a module with identical source text but its imports point to different locations.
If you have two files that both only contain `export const x = 1;`, would those compare identically?
content.formatted_body: If you have two files that both only contain export const x = 1;, would those compare identically?

content.body: right, so provided whitespace and comments match, under the current spec yes
it does also do an equivalence of the URL in addition
Do imports not matter in that case?
import maps would not be part of that identity model
I need to refresh my memory on the module source proposal
this was the longer March presentation - https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1inTcnb4hugyAvKrjFX_XHTnxYaPW0rodWwn0VFmlq24/edit?usp=sharing

content.formatted_body:
I'm actually very surprised that the example in the explainer doesn't maintain source url. If you have

// main.js
import source myModule from "./foo/bar/my-module.js";
const worker = new Worker(myModule);

// foo/bar/my-module.js
import { x } from "../shared.js";

// foo/shared.js
export const x = 1;

The module loaded by the worker would throw because the import can't be resolved?

I'm actually very surprised that the example in the explainer doesn't maintain source url. If you have

// main.js
import source myModule from "./foo/bar/my-module.js";
const worker = new Worker(myModule);

// foo/bar/my-module.js
import { x } from "../shared.js";

// foo/shared.js
export const x = 1;

would the module loaded by the worker throw because the import can't be resolved?

content.body: No, the worker would separately import `/foo/shared.js`. Only `foo/bar/my-module.js` is serialized. Only with module declarations can the whole graph can be serialized. This avoids trying to serialize "instance state" / "linkage state".
content.formatted_body: No, the worker would separately import /foo/shared.js. Only foo/bar/my-module.js is serialized. Only with module declarations can the whole graph can be serialized. This avoids trying to serialize "instance state" / "linkage state".

content.body: Instead `new Worker(myModule)` sets up the worker with the same import map
content.formatted_body: Instead new Worker(myModule) sets up the worker with the same import map

content.body: one outstanding problem for WebAssembly is how to pass the shared memory here though
(likely requires something like Node.js's data argument)
You're saying this resolution occurs during link time? What about dynamic import? It can't statically link `import(somestring)`
content.formatted_body: You're saying this resolution occurs during link time? What about dynamic import? It can't statically link import(somestring)
